quiz.title: Unit 9 Quiz 5, Biology I
quiz.subtitle: Genetics: Advanced Inheritance Patterns and Probability
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Monohybrid Crosses, Incomplete Dominance, Codominance, Blood Types, Dihybrid Crosses, Trihybrid Crosses, Linked Genes, X-Linked Inheritance, Probability Calculations

questions.question: A geneticist crosses a heterozygous brown-eyed person (Bb) with a blue-eyed person (bb). Probability of brown-eyed child is:
questions.explanation: Bb × bb produces 50% Bb (brown eyes) and 50% bb (blue eyes). Brown eyes (dominant) appear in heterozygous offspring.

questions.question: In humans, widow's peak (W) is dominant over straight hairline (w). Cross Ww × Ww. What is the expected phenotypic ratio?
questions.explanation: Ww × Ww produces 3:1 phenotypic ratio (3 widow's peak : 1 straight hairline). The genotypic ratio is 1:2:1 (WW:Ww:ww).

questions.question: In a monohybrid Punnett square, how many different genotypes can be produced from Aa × Aa?
questions.explanation: Aa × Aa produces three genotypes: AA, Aa, and aa. The genotypic ratio is 1:2:1 (1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa).

questions.question: Incomplete dominance occurs when:
questions.explanation: Incomplete dominance results in heterozygotes showing an intermediate phenotype (e.g., pink flowers from red × white). Alleles do not blend permanently and reappear in future generations.

questions.question: Codominance occurs when:
questions.explanation: Codominance means both alleles are fully expressed in heterozygotes. Example: AB blood type where both A and B antigens appear on red blood cells.

questions.question: A cross between red-flowered (RR) and white-flowered (rr) snapdragons produces all pink (Rr) flowers. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Snapdragon flower color shows incomplete dominance: RR = red, Rr = pink, rr = white. Heterozygotes show an intermediate phenotype.

questions.question: Blood group inheritance can be analyzed using Punnett squares. Which blood type shows codominance?
questions.explanation: AB blood type shows codominance because both A and B alleles are fully expressed. Type A and B show complete dominance over O, but A and B are codominant with each other.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Which of the following is true about Punnett squares?
options: They are used only for humans
questions.explanation: Punnett squares show probability of offspring genotypes/phenotypes, not guaranteed outcomes. They can handle dihybrid (4×4) and even trihybrid crosses, and apply to all sexually reproducing organisms.

questions.question: Assuming two genes are unlinked, a dihybrid test cross between AaBb and aabb produces how many distinct phenotypes (with complete dominance)?
questions.explanation: Assuming the genes are unlinked and assort independently, AaBb × aabb produces four phenotype combinations in a 1:1:1:1 ratio: A-B-, A-bb, aaB-, aabb. This is a test cross revealing gamete types from the heterozygous parent.

questions.question: If genes A and B are linked on the same chromosome, a Punnett square assuming independent assortment will:
options: Not apply; recombination frequencies must be used
questions.explanation: Linked genes violate independent assortment. Actual offspring ratios depend on recombination frequency between genes. Standard dihybrid Punnett squares (9:3:3:1) only work for unlinked genes.

questions.question: Assuming all three genes are on different chromosomes and assort independently, in a cross between AaBbCc and AaBbCc (trihybrid), how many genotype classes are possible in offspring?
questions.explanation: Assuming all three genes are on different chromosomes and assort independently, for each gene there are 3 possible genotypes (AA, Aa, aa). Therefore, the total number of genotypic classes is 3 × 3 × 3 = 3³ = 27. Phenotypes with complete dominance = 2³ = 8.

questions.question: Select all that apply: A Punnett square for X-linked recessive inheritance must account for:
options: Different allele frequencies in males and females
Males having only one X chromosome
The fact that males inherit their X chromosome from their mother
That females can be carriers or affected depending on both X chromosomes
questions.explanation: X-linked inheritance requires considering males are hemizygous (one X), males inherit X from mother, and females can be carriers (heterozygous) or affected (homozygous recessive). Allele frequency in a population is irrelevant to a specific cross, and Y-linked patterns are separate.

questions.question: A colorblind man (XᶜY) has children with a carrier woman (XᴺXᶜ). What is the probability their son will be colorblind?
questions.explanation: Sons inherit X from mother and Y from father. Mother (XᴺXᶜ) passes Xᴺ or Xᶜ with 50% probability. If son gets Xᶜ, he's colorblind. So 50% of sons are colorblind.

questions.question: In a dihybrid cross (assuming independent assortment), what fraction of offspring shows the phenotype for one dominant and one recessive trait (e.g., A–bb)?
questions.explanation: In the 9:3:3:1 ratio, 3/16 show A–bb (dominant first trait, recessive second trait). Similarly, 3/16 show aaB– (recessive first, dominant second). This question asks for a single category like A–bb, which is 3/16.

questions.question: In humans, suppose brown eyes (B) are dominant over blue (b) and dark hair (H) is dominant over blonde (h). Cross BbHh × BbHh. Probability of child with blue eyes and blonde hair is:
questions.explanation: Blue eyes = bb (1/4 probability). Blonde hair = hh (1/4 probability). Combined probability = 1/4 × 1/4 = 1/16 for bbhh (both recessive traits).

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered advanced genetics and probability! 🧬
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review incomplete dominance, codominance, and X-linked inheritance.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on dihybrid cross ratios and probability calculations.
scoreMessages.0: Please review Punnett square probabilities, blood type inheritance, and sex-linked traits.
